question.text: Scenario: Mr. Arvind Sharma, aged 42, is a senior manager at an IT company earning Rs. 18 lakh per annum (gross). His wife Mrs. Priya works part-time earning Rs. 6 lakh per annum. Their combined monthly expenses are Rs. 1,20,000. They have two children aged 14 and 10. Arvind's current investments include: Equity Mutual Funds Rs. 35 lakh, PPF Rs. 12 lakh (current balance), Bank Fixed Deposits Rs. 8 lakh, Employee PF balance Rs. 18 lakh, and a savings bank balance of Rs. 3 lakh. He has an outstanding home loan of Rs. 22 lakh with EMI of Rs. 25,000/month (included in expenses above) and no other loans. His employer contributes Rs. 1,800/month to PF and he contributes the same. He wants to retire at age 58 and needs a retirement corpus of Rs. 3 crore. He also needs Rs. 30 lakh for his elder child's education in 4 years and Rs. 25 lakh for the younger child in 8 years. Assume equity returns at 12% CAGR and debt returns at 7% CAGR. Inflation is 6% p.a. Question 1 of 5: What is the Sharma family's current total investment portfolio value (excluding home)?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Total investments = Equity MFs (Rs. 35 lakh) + PPF (Rs. 12 lakh) + FDs (Rs. 8 lakh) + Employee PF (Rs. 18 lakh) + Savings bank (Rs. 3 lakh) = Rs. 76 lakh. **The Trap:** Rs. 54 lakh excludes PF balance — but PF is the employee's money even though not immediately accessible. Rs. 72 lakh might miss the savings bank balance. **Cross-Exam Context:** Portfolio valuation across asset classes is tested in X-A, X-B, and XXI-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always include PF/NPS balances in total portfolio assessment — many clients underestimate their total savings by ignoring employer-managed retirement accounts.
